exercises.title: SQLite In-Memory Database: Create and Insert
exercises.description: Use sqlite3 with an in-memory database (':memory:') to create a table, insert rows, and query data. Practice the core CRUD operations.
exercises.starterCode:
import sqlite3

# TODO: Connect to in-memory database
# conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')

# TODO: Create a 'students' table with columns:
# id (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT), name (TEXT), gpa (REAL), year (INTEGER)

# TODO: Insert these students:
# ('Alice', 3.9, 2024), ('Bob', 3.5, 2023), ('Carol', 3.7, 2024)
# ('Dave', 3.2, 2022), ('Eve', 3.8, 2023)
# Use executemany() with a list of tuples

# TODO: Query and print all students sorted by gpa descending
# Format: "1. Alice (GPA: 3.90, Year: 2024)"

# TODO: Query and print count of students per year
# Format: "Year 2022: 1 students"

# TODO: Close the connection
exercises.expectedOutput:
1. Alice (GPA: 3.90, Year: 2024)
2. Eve (GPA: 3.80, Year: 2023)
3. Carol (GPA: 3.70, Year: 2024)
4. Bob (GPA: 3.50, Year: 2023)
5. Dave (GPA: 3.20, Year: 2022)
Year 2022: 1 students
Year 2023: 2 students
Year 2024: 2 students
hints: conn.execute('CREATE TABLE students (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, name TEXT, gpa REAL, year INTEGER)')
conn.executemany('INSERT INTO students (name, gpa, year) VALUES (?, ?, ?)', data)
cursor = conn.execute('SELECT * FROM students ORDER BY gpa DESC')
conn.execute('SELECT year, COUNT(*) FROM students GROUP BY year ORDER BY year')

exercises.title: SQLite: Update, Delete, and Transactions
exercises.description: Practice UPDATE and DELETE operations in SQLite in-memory DB. Also demonstrate transactions: batch inserts succeed together or fail together.
exercises.starterCode:
import sqlite3

conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
conn.execute('''
    CREATE TABLE products (
        id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
        name TEXT NOT NULL,
        price REAL,
        stock INTEGER DEFAULT 0
    )
''')

# Insert initial data
products = [
    ('Laptop', 999.99, 10),
    ('Mouse', 29.99, 50),
    ('Keyboard', 79.99, 30),
    ('Monitor', 399.99, 15),
    ('Headset', 149.99, 20),
]
conn.executemany('INSERT INTO products (name, price, stock) VALUES (?, ?, ?)', products)
conn.commit()

# TODO: Update: Apply 10% discount to all products with price > 100
# Use: UPDATE products SET price = price * 0.9 WHERE price > 100
# Print: "Updated N rows"

# TODO: Delete: Remove products with stock > 40
# Print: "Deleted N rows"

# TODO: Query and print remaining products sorted by price
# Format: "Mouse: $29.99 (stock: 50)"

# TODO: Print total inventory value (sum of price * stock)
# Format: "Total inventory value: $XXXXX.XX"

conn.close()
exercises.expectedOutput:
Updated 4 rows
Deleted 1 rows
Keyboard: $79.99 (stock: 30)
Headset: $134.99 (stock: 20)
Monitor: $359.99 (stock: 15)
Laptop: $899.99 (stock: 10)
Total inventory value: $19499.30
hints: cursor = conn.execute('UPDATE products SET price = price * 0.9 WHERE price > 100')
cursor = conn.execute('DELETE FROM products WHERE stock > 40')
conn.execute('SELECT name, price, stock FROM products ORDER BY price')

exercises.title: SQLite: Row Factory and Complex Queries
exercises.description: Use sqlite3.Row as a row_factory to access columns by name. Practice JOIN-like operations using multiple tables and complex aggregate queries.
exercises.starterCode:
import sqlite3

conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row  # Enable column access by name

# Create schema
conn.executescript('''
    CREATE TABLE departments (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT);
    CREATE TABLE employees (
        id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
        name TEXT,
        dept_id INTEGER,
        salary REAL,
        FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES departments(id)
    );

    INSERT INTO departments VALUES (1, 'Engineering'), (2, 'Marketing'), (3, 'HR');
    INSERT INTO employees VALUES
        (1, 'Alice', 1, 95000),
        (2, 'Bob', 2, 72000),
        (3, 'Carol', 1, 88000),
        (4, 'Dave', 3, 65000),
        (5, 'Eve', 1, 102000),
        (6, 'Frank', 2, 78000);
''')

# TODO: Query employees with their department name using JOIN
# SELECT e.name, d.name as dept, e.salary
# FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id
# ORDER BY e.salary DESC
# Print: "Alice | Engineering | $95000.00"

print()

# TODO: Query average salary per department
# SELECT d.name, AVG(e.salary), COUNT(e.id)
# FROM departments d JOIN employees e ON d.id = e.dept_id
# GROUP BY d.name ORDER BY d.name
# Print: "Engineering: avg=$95000.00, headcount=3"

conn.close()
exercises.expectedOutput:
Eve | Engineering | $102000.00
Alice | Engineering | $95000.00
Carol | Engineering | $88000.00
Frank | Marketing | $78000.00
Bob | Marketing | $72000.00
Dave | HR | $65000.00

Engineering: avg=$95000.00, headcount=3
HR: avg=$65000.00, headcount=1
Marketing: avg=$75000.00, headcount=2
hints: conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row  # rows accessible by column name
SELECT d.name, AVG(e.salary) as avg_sal, COUNT(e.id) as cnt FROM departments d JOIN employees e ON d.id = e.dept_id GROUP BY d.name ORDER BY d.name

warmup.title: Quick Recall: SQLite Basics
warmup.description: What does this code print? Trace the SQLite in-memory operations.
warmup.starterCode:
import sqlite3

conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
conn.execute('CREATE TABLE t (x INTEGER, y TEXT)')
conn.execute("INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'a')")
conn.execute("INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 'b')")
conn.commit()

rows = conn.execute('SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(x) FROM t').fetchone()
print(rows[0])
print(rows[1])
conn.close()
